1: 'He called in my hearing with a loud voice: Bring forward those who carry out punishment against the city, each man with his destroying weapon in his hand' — the six judgment-executors summoned. The loud voice calling the executors is the same calling that will send the angels at the eschaton: 'He will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds' (Matthew 24:31). The angels of judgment in Ezekiel 9 are the type of the eschatological harvesters of Matthew 13:39-42.
2: 'Six men came from the direction of the upper gate, which faces north, each with his deadly weapon in his hand; and with them was a man clothed in linen, with a writing kit at his side' — the man in linen with a writing kit among the six executors. The man in linen stands apart from the destroyers — he is a scribe, a sealer, not an executioner. This figure of the divine scribe who marks the faithful for preservation before the executors advance anticipates the sealing of the 144,000 before the angels of judgment are released (Revelation 7:1-3).
3: 'Now the glory of the God of Israel had gone up from above the cherubim, where it had been, and moved to the threshold of the temple. Then the LORD called to the man clothed in linen who had the writing kit at his side' — the glory moving to the threshold is the first stage of the departure (Ezekiel 10 will record the full departure). Before the judgment begins, the glory repositions — a divine withdrawal that makes the judgment possible and signals its authority. The command to the man in linen comes directly from the glory at the threshold.
4: 'Go throughout the city of Jerusalem and put a mark on the foreheads of those who grieve and lament over all the detestable things that are done in it' — the tav-mark on the foreheads of the faithful remnant. The mark identifies those who mourn over sin while surrounded by it — the community of conscience within a corrupt society. This mark is directly echoed in: the seal of the Holy Spirit (2 Corinthians 1:22; Ephesians 1:13-14; Ezekiel 4:30), the sealing of the 144,000 (Revelation 7:3), the name of God on the foreheads of the redeemed (Revelation 14:1; Ezekiel 22:4). The seal that preserves in judgment is the Spirit of Christ, given to those who belong to him.
5: 'As I listened, he said to the others: Go throughout the city after him and kill, without showing pity or compassion' — the executors follow the scribe: the judgment comes only after the sealing. This sequence — marking the faithful first, then judgment — is the consistent pattern of divine judgment throughout Scripture: Passover blood on the doorposts before the angel of death passes through; Noah sealed in the ark before the flood; Rahab's scarlet thread before Jericho's fall. Christ's death is the ultimate prior sealing before eschatological judgment.
6: 'Slaughter old men, young men and maidens, women and children, but do not touch anyone who has the mark' — the comprehensive scope of judgment contrasted with the absolute protection of the marked. 'Begin at my sanctuary' — judgment starting at the house of God. Peter quotes this directly: 'For it is time for judgment to begin with the household of God; and if it begins with us, what will be the outcome for those who do not obey the gospel of God?' (1 Peter 4:17). The sanctuary-first judgment is the pattern for the church's experience of discipline before the world faces judgment.
7: 'Defile the temple and fill the courts with the slain. Go!' — the defilement of the temple by the slain — corpses rendering the sacred space impure. The deaths that defile the temple are the consequence of the idolatry that had already spiritually defiled it (Ezekiel 8). Jesus's prediction of the temple's physical destruction (Matthew 24:2) is the final form of this judgment: the physically destroyed temple as the terminus of the spiritually destroyed temple. The new temple is Christ's body, which cannot be defiled by death because death cannot hold it (Acts 2:24).
8: 'While they were killing and I was left alone, I fell facedown, crying out: Alas, Sovereign LORD! Are you going to destroy the entire remnant of Israel in this outpouring of your wrath on Jerusalem?' — Ezekiel's intercession on behalf of Israel during the judgment vision. The prophet prostrate before YHWH, crying out for the remnant, is the OT type of Christ's high-priestly intercession: 'He always lives to intercede for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The one who has seen the full extent of the judgment becomes the intercessor who cries for the remnant.
9: 'He answered me: The sin of Israel and Judah is exceedingly great; the land is full of bloodshed and the city is full of injustice. They say, The LORD has forsaken the land; the LORD does not see' — the divine confirmation of the full extent of the sin that Ezekiel witnessed in the temple tour. The 'LORD has forsaken/does not see' is the precise theology that enabled the sin: conviction of divine absence enabling human autonomy. The cross answers this directly: it is the place where God appeared most absent ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — Matthew 27:46) and most present (2 Corinthians 5:19: God was in Christ reconciling the world).
10: 'So I will not look on them with pity or spare them, but I will bring down on their own heads what they have done' — the principle of exact recompense. Christ absorbs the exact recompense that would have fallen on sinners: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The 'bringing down on their own heads what they have done' is precisely what did not happen to the believer because it happened at the cross.
11: 'Then the man in linen with the writing kit came back and reported, I have done as you commanded' — the scribe's return report after completing the marking. The mission accomplished. The completeness of the sealing before judgment began means not one who should have been sealed was missed. In Christ, the seal of the Spirit is equally comprehensive: 'Having believed, you were marked in him with a seal, the promised Holy Spirit' (Ephesians 1:13). The divine scribe misses no one who belongs to him.
